data.title: StoneCo: The Recent Drop Is A Golden Buying Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered StoneCo (STNE), I highlighted their robust growth and strong financials, trading at an attractive valuation despite their significant potential to develop in Banking and Credit and even in the region over the longer term.

With the valuation getting more attractive following the recent price drop and the potential to see nearly 30% in shareholder returns in 2026 following the sale of their Linx business, STNE is upgraded to a Strong Buy.

Excellent Growth Continues

Despite a solid year with revenue growing by ~13%, STNE dropped significantly on the day of the release, recognizing a ~$30.6 million (BRL 157.8 million) loss from the Linx divestment and an expense of $9.4 million (BRL 48.5 million) for settlements related to civil lawsuits, while TPV growth was also below expectations, signaling a slowdown in merchant transactions - although the Adjusted EPS grew by 33.6% YoY.

StoneCo’s guidance for 2026 remained within their guidance, with a midpoint in Adjusted Basic EPS of $2.15 per share (BRL 11.1), while the 2027 level was cut to a midpoint of $2.44 (BRL 12.6), although the 2027 guidance assumes no impact from their potentially massive buybacks that can continue, while the one for 2026 doesn’t include the potential special distributions that I’ll mention below, which might be a significant understatement that opens the potential for a beat.

STNE also announced that the new managerial total capital ratio will drop from the previous safety cushion of at least 20% to 17%, which unlocks even more cash for the company to spend on growth or shareholder returns, being the result of their enhanced methodology, aiming to match the official standards used by the Brazilian central bank.

After running the company for ~3 years and executing several strategic initiatives (divesting non-core assets such as Linx, streamlining the business, introducing a capital allocation framework, and continuing the company's evolution), STNE's CEO announced his resignation back in early January, being replaced by the CFO, with the ex-CEO highlighting the following during their Q4 Earnings Call, potentially highlighting continuity in this direction:

I have complete confidence in Mateus' leadership. He has been one of the architects of Stone's transformation, helping restore discipline, simplify the business and focus the organization on what truly matters. He brings clarity of direction, analytical rigor and the right sense of urgency to accelerate execution and continue elevating what matters most, delivering value to our clients and building intrinsic value per share.

Financially, based on STNE’s latest report, they remain in a very strong position, with current assets covering their total liabilities and a manageable amount of debt despite increasing YoY. Again, this is distorted by their banking segment that’s growing significantly, but they continue to aim for an adjusted net cash position, with their adjusted net cash from continuing operations reaching $457 million (BRL 2.36 billion) in Q4.

Based on 2025's performance, the company expects to spend over $390 million (BRL 2 billion) on shareholder returns in the form of buybacks, which represents a buyback yield of >11.40% based on the current market cap of $3.42 billion, further showing the fact that the management - who also has plenty of exposure to the business as shareholders - continues to see the value in their stock.

Meanwhile, thanks to the Linx sale receiving the needed approvals, the company also expects to return an additional ~$600 million (BRL 3.08 billion) to shareholders, planning to define the distribution mechanism in April, meaning that we’re basically looking at a total yield of nearly 30% in 2026.

Rate Cut Hopes vs. Iran Uncertainty

One of the main, major developments that can boost STNE from a company and a stock point of view can be the potential Selic cuts expected this year, after the Brazilian Central Bank held rates at very elevated levels for the past few years, with the first of the long-awaited cuts being officially announced on March 18, although it was only 25 basis points (to 14.75% from 15.00%) instead of the hoped-for 50, as a result of the ongoing uncertainty in the Middle East, despite inflation reportedly cooling down before it.

This can be great for a company like STNE, as these cuts (with expectations now ranging from about 11.75% to 13.00% by the end of 2026) can potentially offer a boost in economic activity, as rate hikes slow down the economy to fight inflation, while cuts are theoretically doing the opposite. The company’s management also highlighted the following regarding it:

So basically, we're assuming Selic at low 12% by the end of 2026 and high 11% for 2027. That's what's behind the guidance number. On the credit side, basically, what you have is the result of a portfolio that keeps growing into different publics and different products, but also getting more mature, especially on the core product for the digital credit offerings that we have. So let me try to walk you through a bit of some of those moving parts.

Meanwhile, the USD gained as a result of a flight to safety due to the Iran conflict, but the BRL held relatively steady despite an initial jump, as the country can also win from the war premiums in their commodities, potentially highlighting their potential in the long term as a solid tool to hedge against this type of macro uncertainty - with Petrobras (PBR) nearly doubling YTD, making it one of the best performers in the oil and gas industry.

However, this conflict can have significant effects on these rate cut expectations. Although Brazil can win from this since the country is a major commodity producer, the potential inflationary shock as a result of a major jump in core raw materials/energy prices can also take its toll on the Central Bank of Brazil’s decisions surrounding interest rates.

This was highlighted by the Fed during Powell’s latest speech, where he announced that the Fed wouldn’t cut and warned about the potential inflationary shock, while the ECB chief said they’re ready to hike rates as soon as possible while warning about the potentially underestimated effects that Iran’s retaliatory attacks had on the region’s infrastructure, potentially leading to months or even more until flows would come back to normal.

Inflation (following the pandemic and the subsequent money printing) was the main reason why we had these rate hikes in the first place, and there’s certainly the possibility to see them pressure Brazil as well for longer. However, I’ll also highlight that there’s a lot of room for improvements in the economy since the country can get back on the long-term trend of rate reductions, potentially offering a long-term fundamental boost to its economic activity while also pushing money away from government bonds into equity, with STNE being a solid candidate for high growth that’s also reflecting the economy it depends on.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, STNE will depend on the environment by default, while the ongoing uncertainty and potential pressure caused by the Iran conflict can add a lot of volatility and pressure on their valuation.

Meanwhile, competition remains a very significant issue to keep in mind, as STNE is far from the only player that’s there to capitalize on the region’s digitalization growth potential, which can pressure margins and potential to develop in the long term, even though I continue to consider Brazil the pillar of their future expansion in the region, which in itself comes with specific risks.

Valuation

For STNE’s DCF to Equity, to get to the FCFE equivalent, since we’re talking about a financial business, I’ll adjust their Adj. Net Income of $480 million is seen in 2025 with a retention rate (i.e., the CAGR divided by the ROE) of 40% throughout the entire period to account for the money that basically funds their growth, getting to an FCFE of $288 million in 2025.

Based on the current 248.9 million number of shares, that’s an FCFE/share of $1.157 in 2025, and given the implied CAGR they estimate in their EPS from 2025 to 2027, I'll use 20% as a conservative estimate to account for the potential for buybacks to boost this beyond their expected ~13.92% based on the guidance, followed by a 12% CAGR until 2030 and 8% until 2035, despite the potential for an international expansion that can continue keeping this in the double-digit range (especially if combined with massive buybacks), with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a 12.5% discount rate to take into account the competitive and general macro risks for the region.

This way, we get to an equity value of $5.74 billion, for an intrinsic value of $23.08 per share, which remains well above the current $13.95 at the moment of writing this, and that’s when using relatively conservative estimates.

Conclusions

STNE is upgraded to Strong Buy, with the valuation getting to a level that’s very hard to ignore while they stand to benefit significantly from potential macro improvements and an expansion, despite recent pressure.

There are risks to keep in mind given the industry and the country, but the potential for a long-term international pivot, massive buybacks that can continue since they’re funded by their cash flow, on top of the region’s digitalization trends.

data.title: Capturing Consistent Return Streams In Capricious Equity Markets
data.description:
Originally Published on March 26, 2026

By Joshua Lisser

How can an active core equity portfolio deliver more reliable long-term results from a modest alpha target?

For much of the past decade, equity investing has been defined by abundance. Strong beta, sustained momentum and a narrow group of market leaders meant that returns often came easily, just by being invested. When markets delivered double-digit gains, consistency was largely taken for granted.

That backdrop is changing. Return expectations are lower, market leadership is more concentrated, and factor and style rotations have become more volatile. For many active managers, beating benchmarks consistently has become more difficult—and taking large, high-conviction positions increasingly risky.

So is there a way to reap the benefits of active management without assuming undue risks? Instead of searching for the next outsize opportunity, we think investors should look for portfolios designed to deliver repeatable results across different market regimes. In a lower-return world, even modest excess returns can meaningfully improve long-term outcomes, and consistency will become an increasingly valuable asset in its own right.

Style Volatility Raises Performance Hurdles

Consistently beating a benchmark is harder than ever. In recent years, the MSCI World Index outperformed 67% of actively managed funds in the eVestment Global Blend Universe (Display). To some extent, this trend was driven by the dominance of the US mega stocks, which complicated efforts by active equity managers to deviate from the benchmark without taking on too much risk.

Active Managers Have Struggled Amid Increased Style Volatility

Style volatility has also raised hurdles to performance. Although we strongly believe traditional style-based investing, such as growth and value equities, has historically offered long-term return benefits, investors must often cope with long periods of underperformance along the way. Over the last 10 years, the dispersion of factor index returns widened significantly compared with 2005–2015, meaning investors paid a heavy penalty for choosing the wrong style at the wrong time.

Combining Fundamental Insights and Quantitative Controls

So how can investors overcome these challenges? As we see it, the key to consistency is to create an active portfolio that combines the benefits of broad fundamental research and quantitative tools.

This approach is based on a simple philosophy. Skilled fundamental analysts are great at identifying undervalued securities that can outperform over the long term. Quantitative risk controls can help manage portfolio risk and neutralize factor tilts, which supports more consistent results. In other words, this type of strategy can isolate high-conviction active positions while deploying quantitative tools to optimize a portfolio and ultimately overcome the erratic style winds that may blow a portfolio off course.

Fundamental Research: Company Expertise Still Matters

Style risk feels particularly acute today. The rapid growth of artificial intelligence has driven both the outsized returns of hyperscalers and adjacent names and has punished companies perceived to be at risk of disruption, notably those in enterprise software. Heightened geopolitical stresses have also added to this cocktail of risk. We think deep company research can pave the way to differentiated positions in broader parts of the market. But sharpening stockpicking in a core equity portfolio requires access to a large pool of fundamental research across the style spectrum.

Skilled value, growth and core equity managers have expertise in different investing disciplines. Value investors search for underappreciated shares of companies with quality businesses and improvement prospects by focusing on the disconnect between fundamentals and valuation. Growth portfolios target accelerating and profitable growth, while core equity investors look for steady compounders derived from resilient long-term cash flows. Tapping into fundamental research across styles enables an equity strategy to choose the highest-conviction holdings with complementary features that can provide balance through changing factor regimes.

Quantitative Building Blocks for Portfolio Construction

But sourcing an array of fundamentally attractive holdings doesn’t provide adequate risk control on its own. Quantitative research can make the difference by addressing critical questions.

What are the expected returns for all portfolio candidates and holdings? How do securities sourced across the market diversify one another? How can we limit unintended or uncompensated risks in the portfolio? Does the strategy’s risk budget warrant constraints on specific position sizes?

Quantitative models can answer these questions and apply them to portfolio construction. Well-designed quantitative tools can help limit factor exposures and augment returns from security selection while calibrating weights to broaden risk. The model can be designed to optimize holdings and create a well-structured equity portfolio.

A Sturdier Foundation for Smoother Return Patterns

This framework supports steadier investment outcomes in three ways.

First, it can construct a portfolio with index-like characteristics. That means there are no big sector, country, or factor tilts, which often fuel volatility.

Second, limiting a portfolio’s vulnerability to those biases allows stock selection to be the primary return driver.

Third, taken together, this structure supports a relatively modest excess return target at a similarly modest tracking error. Instead of shooting for the stars, we think this type of strategy can beat the benchmark without suffering wild swings that may undermine confidence in turbulent markets. In fact, our research suggests that core equity portfolios with lower tracking error have outperformed over time (Display).

Low Relative Risk Hits the Sweet Spot in Core Equity Portfolios

Where Does the Strategy Fit?

These benefits create a portfolio with a distinctive place in an equity allocation that can complement passive or active portfolios for distinct investing needs.

While passive portfolios have become popular in recent years, many investors are concerned about market concentration risks and more modest returns moving forward. If US mega-cap dominance wanes and market returns broaden, passive portfolios with heavy weights in the technology titans could suffer. In a lower equity market return environment, the opportunity to generate alpha may become significantly more important than it has been in years past. Passive investors can prepare for a scenario like this by adding a strategy with modest alpha potential.

Concentration has also created a conundrum for investors in traditional active equity strategies, many of whom have suffered disappointing relative returns. Investors who believe in the power of active stockpicking but want to reduce the risk of underperformance can swap out some of an active allocation toward a strategy that aims to generate consistent outperformance while reducing risk.

Generating reliable return patterns is becoming increasingly valuable. When executed with discipline, this type of equity portfolio can offer the best of both worlds: passive-like risk characteristics with a moderate but significant dollop of alpha that compounds nicely over time. In today’s markets, that type of consistency isn’t a consolation prize—it’s a foundation for better long‑term outcomes.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations, and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any MSCI data contained herein. The MSCI data may not be further redistributed or used as a basis for other indices or any securities or financial products. This report is not approved, reviewed or produced by MSCI.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Conagra: I Am Buying This Value (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I last wrote about Conagra Brands, Inc. (CAG) last September. In that article called Conagra: The 7%+ Dividend Yield Is Safe… For now, I focused on the safety of the dividend and rated the stock as a hold. When that article was published, the stock was priced at $19.17 per share, and it currently sits at $15.72—a drop of 18%.

Has the situation gotten worse for CAG? Or is it a bargain now?

In my opinion, the stock is a bargain. I had a small holding back in September, and I have added considerably to it while the stock has been in the $15 and change area.

In this article, I'll go over some of the metrics that I looked at before, but with less of a focus on the dividend and more of a focus on valuation.

The Dividend

Let's get the dividend out of the way right off the bat. The yield is now close to a monster 9%. If that yield is safe, then that alone is a strong argument for owning the company.

Since my article in September, CAG has paid out two quarterly dividends of $0.35 (in line with previous) with ex-dividend dates of October 30 and January 27. Last week, CAG announced another $0.35 dividend with an ex-dividend date of April 30.

So the company has paid out for two quarters since my declaration of safety back in September and is about to pay out a third. So far, so good.

Going forward, the company has guided for earnings of $1.70 in fiscal 2026 (ending in May), and analysts expect $1.71 in fiscal 2027. These numbers have come down slightly and are a little bit lower than what I was calling for. The reason is inflation. CAG is susceptible to tariffs (particularly on metals) and energy prices. They hedge for these costs, but not 100%.

Nonetheless, if CAG can hang on to those projections, covering the $1.40 per year dividend is achievable. In my opinion, the dividend is still safe.

The following chart shows just how juicy the dividend is:

Valuation

Looking at the usual metrics, we can see right away that Conagra is rather cheap compared to historical numbers. Here are P/E and P/S charts:

The forward P/E is a mere 9.25.

The P/S chart is interesting to me. I went back 30 years ago on this one, and at first glance, the reader might opine that the stock is simply back where it should be. It was at the current P/S level for about 10 years, from 2005 to 2015.

However, there is a key difference between then and now. I highlighted the operating margin in my previous CAG article, and that is still a metric that I consider to be of the utmost importance. In the last earnings update, management guided for an operating margin “near the high end” of 11.0% and 11.5%. I will assume 11.3%.

Here is the historical chart. Pay close attention to the 2005-2015 period and compare it to the current assumption of 11.3%.

It can readily be seen that margins were considerably lower the last time the CAG was selling for a P/S number of 0.67. If one were just to look at the P/S chart, it would be easy to think that today's value is not particularly extreme. However, due to the lower margins in that 2005-2015 time frame, the P/E was generally around 12-15. With the higher margins today, I value the company at a higher P/S than back then and a higher P/E than the 9.25 forward number that it currently sports.

I would rather not pile on metric after metric here. The story is the same. Conagra is about as cheap as it has ever been, however one chooses to measure it. The dividend yield is extremely high and, in my opinion, safe for now.

Sentiment for the entire packaged food industry is very low these days. It can feel like these companies must be doomed. However, a look at CAG shows that the business, while facing challenges, is still cranking out decent profits and should not be valued as if it were imploding.

Target Price

My target for the stock, assuming operating margins can hold above 11%, corresponds to a forward P/E of 11 (see below for my reasoning).

11 x $1.7 = $18.7.

If the stock rises to that level and nothing substantial changes, I would still consider it an easy hold. The dividend yield would be 7.5%.

Risks

The risks to the company are real. I alluded to the most important ones above:

1) Input costs (which are sensitive to tariffs).

2) Energy costs (which are sensitive to geopolitical uncertainties).

These are very unpredictable factors, and owning a CAG is not for everyone. It is not a SWAN (Sleep Well at Night) stock.

The risks outlined are the reason that my target price is based on a forward P/E of 11. While the past P/E figures were almost always higher than 11, margins are harder to predict now and could very well drop, which would then lead to less earnings and could lead to a dividend cut.

Conclusion

Conagra is in a spot where a contrarian investor should seriously consider it. Sentiment is low. Valuation is low. Expectations are low.

If the stock bounces back to my target price in 12 months (note that it was at that price one month ago), then buyers are looking at a gain of 28%. If the stock goes nowhere and the dividend holds up, then buyers are looking at a gain of 9%.

Option strategies also work well with CAG. I have sold some covered calls on it to increase my return, with the idea that it is unlikely to suddenly jump up a lot. If my calls are exercised, then I am perfectly happy, and I am unlikely to have missed out on a big gain.

I didn't want to go too deep into the possible option strategies here, but I'll throw out one to consider: May 15 calls at 16 strike. They are bidding $0.45. If one were to buy at $15.72 and sell those calls, a dividend of $0.35 would be reaped, as well as the option premium. That's 80 cents for a 40-day investment. If the stock goes nowhere, the investor has made 5% (45% annualized). If it gets exercised, so much the better.

data.title: How Higher Oil Prices Are Complicating The Outlook For Rates And The Economy
data.description:
As oil prices stay elevated, the path forward for the Bank of Canada and the U.S. Fed is getting murkier. Derek Burleton, Deputy Chief Economist at TD Bank Group, discusses the impact of higher energy costs on monetary policy and whether it’s creating an inflation problem or a growth problem for Canada’s economy.

Kim Parlee: The price of oil continues to remain at elevated levels as the Middle East conflict carries into its fifth week. And that's leading some to take a sober second look at the state of inflation, interest rates, and economic impact, including here in Canada. Here to give us a sense of what it all means is Derek Burleton of TD Bank Group.

It is great to have you here.

Derek Burleton: Thank you.

Kim Parlee: I want to start, if I could, with the really big picture. If I didn't know anything about what was happening in the world right now, and I ask you what impact does the Middle East conflict and all the things that happened because of it have on the Canadian economy going forward and maybe the US economy going forward, what would you say?

Derek Burleton: Well, I would first say the nature of the shock. It is a supply shock. And now, when you think back, since 2020, we've had four to five supply shocks. The pandemic, even some of the tariffs being introduced by Trump, that throws a wrinkle in supply chains.

What it can do is drive up inflation. As prices go up, it can trim economic growth forecasts. And that's exactly what we're dealing with with this kind of plain vanilla-type oil shock. It's leading to inflation worries. It's leading to a lot of movement in the bond markets as yields go up to reflect those inflation worries.

Secondary right now-- and it's interesting-- are the growth-related worries. I think right now, just given the starting point, particularly the United States economy doing very well on a spending front with all the AI investment and inflation holding up high to enter into this crisis, the worry is that inflation that's lofty could be even more lofty.

So that, right now, is a worry. Unfortunately, we have no certainty around how long the war is going to last, how much damage there is to infrastructure. And as we get more answers to that, it'll be a little bit easier to decipher how high inflation will go in this environment.

Kim Parlee: Not to be indelicate, but when you think about Canada-- and correct me if I'm wrong-- I believe Canada is a net exporter of energy, I know that, but also the States, I think, is also a net exporter. And putting the humanitarian disaster aside, is there an economic benefit with higher oil prices to parts of Canada?

Derek Burleton: Oh, there is, absolutely. And we were just talking about Alberta's budget assumed a $60 barrel of oil. And oil running much higher than that means it's a gusher of government revenues.

Again, one can guesstimate how long this is going to last. Even if it only lasted a month or two, it would create billions in additional revenue this year. That money gets spent. Some of that money gets channeled through the federal government through higher revenue as well, and that gets redistributed.

So clearly, the winners are the oil sector - which will, even in the short run, I see some scope to increase production. Unfortunately, not all parts of Canada rely directly on the oil and gas. So, it'll be-- the uncertainty doesn't help spending and investment. So, the two offset each other, to some extent.

I think many forecasts say any oil price spike is a net positive for the Canadian economy. I'm not so sure. I would view it more or less being offsetting to start, and that to a certain point, if oil prices keep going up, it could potentially turn into a net negative and we could end up chopping Canada's forecast quite significantly.

Kim Parlee: Let's talk about then-- I want to start with some of the negative. Again, you said high oil prices can lead to inflation. It gets fed through everything else.

But just on, I'll say, the impact of inflation, the demand destruction that happens, how long do we need to see high oil prices for that to really start to take effect? Especially when I think about Ontario and the ones that are not benefiting from high oil prices.

Derek Burleton: You know, we've run a lot of different scenarios. And even if prices were to stay, say for WTI, above $100 a barrel, say 105, 100-105 even for a couple of months, we'd be lowering our growth forecasts, raising near-term inflation. But I don't think we'd have a recession.

I think, again, the positives would just be enough to outweigh. There'd likely be government fiscal response even beyond what we're already seeing, relief to households and businesses affected and the like. And I think we'd avoid it.

But you get to an even higher level, say if the prices broke above-- say the Hormuz is closed well into late April. Prices, say, drive up to 150. Then I think we may be at a tipping point, where all of a sudden the Fed or the Bank of Canada are now looking more at recession possibilities. And that could mean also a pivot for interest rates. We're talking about maybe the possibility they may have to raise rates, or in the case of the Fed, hold them stable.

Kim Parlee: To deal with inflation, yeah.

Derek Burleton: Just to ensure inflation expectations don't become unanchored. But you hit a certain point, and they're like, well, we'd better ease because we may be having an even bigger problem down the road in terms of weak growth.

Kim Parlee: Hm. It's funny, when you say late April, like, that's not that far away.

Derek Burleton: It isn't. And again, it's so hard to guesstimate because we know there are offsets underway, releases of Strategic Petroleum Reserves, some rediverting in Saudi production. Inventories were soft to start the shock. That helps a bit.

So, it gets a bit hard to guesstimate what could drive that 150. But I wouldn't be surprised. I think there's a bit of consensus running around, a bit of crowd-thinking forming that-- say it were to last a whole other month from now, then yeah, you could be breaching pretty high levels, something north of 150.

Kim Parlee: And again, we're all, I'll say, digesting the news in real time, and things change. You could have the US withdraw. You could still have the Strait of Hormuz closed. So there's different iterations.

Derek Burleton: Many different permutations on that. That's one angle. And then you could get some de-escalation as well, there's no doubt.

Kim Parlee: What is your outlook, again sticking to Canada, given the real estate outlook? I think you've recently downgraded your real estate outlook across Canada.

Derek Burleton: We did. And this is part of Canada's growth story, I think, right now. Canada entered the shock with kind of a 1% growth outlook for 2026. Not great. US is still north of 2%. 1% we've been stuck with for a little while, so not a ton of cushion.

And part of that story-- there are a few different headwinds. There's the weaker population, where actually we saw the first decline in Canadian population since Confederation. And that--

Kim Parlee: Really?

Derek Burleton: Yeah, reduces the number of consumers in the economy-- an area that had been really greasing the wheels through '23 and '24. So that is now reversing. But tied to that is the housing market. And not all housing markets are created equally in Canada. We've got the GTA housing market, in particular, is undergoing a lot of pain. It's now, what are we, three years into a down cycle.

The GVA, British Columbia markets are also struggling, albeit not quite to the same extent. And then you look outside of that, things are brighter. I mean, anything east of Toronto into Quebec, Atlantic are still faring well. And then Calgary has been softening of late in Edmonton.

But I come back to that GTA market, which is really struggling. And I think when I look at price indices, we're now three years into a correction. It's very much mapping what we saw in the early '90s. That was a five- to six-year correction.

Now, that is a slow correction. I think the alternative would have been, just rip the Band-Aid off and see a huge decline in prices. But that wouldn't be anybody's interest. So this is actually, of the two, it's maybe a little bit better. Unfortunately, it's going to lengthen out.

So, some of the measures we've seen-- the Ontario government announced PST relief for new home purchases, on top of some of the easing in development charges. That will help the new sector a little bit. Some of that may rob from existing home demand because it increased the incentives to buy new homes.

But overall, I think net is a bit of a positive. But there's no silver bullet with this kind of affordability unraveling that we've needed to see a correction. And I see maybe next year a stability, but it's going to be another 12-18 months of softness, I think.

Kim Parlee: Derek, we've been talking about the impact of the Middle East on Canada and all the ways it impacts everyone in the world. But we do have some special dynamics here that make it unique. One thing, of course, we have coming up is the renegotiation of CUSMA, our trade agreement with the United States. How does the Middle East conflict impact that?

Derek Burleton: Well, it doesn't help negotiations. It's a big distraction. That said, the Minister of Trade the other day was just saying that talks are still going on. They resumed after a bit of a brief pause. Was tight-lipped on what exactly they're talking about.

Clearly ahead of this, there was some talk about maybe the US reducing some of the sectoral tariffs on Canada in areas like steel and aluminum in return for-- and the US has a list of irritants, including procurement. The list goes on, right? Supply-managed alcohol bans by provinces. They've got their list of seven, eight things. So, those talks were underway.

I think the other path of, of course, stream would be on the trilateral part of the deal, which is much bigger with Mexico involved. And I don't think a lot is going on on that front.

So let's just put it this way. We, in our latest forecast, didn't make any heroic assumptions on USMCA. I still think there's a very high probability this gets punted into 2027. If we don't have any review that results in renewal by July, then it turns to annual reviews. If we do, then we could have a 16-year deal and they won't have to review it. But I do think it'll turn to annual reviews, which isn't great for certainty if you're an investor. But I just think that's in all likelihood.

It doesn't seem a huge amount of urgency, given low tariffs on Canada. There's a USMCA carveout that protects Canadian exporters, to some extent. And I think given that, I think Canada is OK to live within the current frame, and the US as well. So that's probably the most likely--

Kim Parlee: Does Canada not get a little bit more of maybe strategic leverage with the Middle East conflict in the conversation? I mean, we have a lot of oil. We send a lot of oil to the States. And I would also even think, with the tariffs that are going on to the sectoral tariffs you talked about, that's inflationary for the US, which again is going to be dealing with as well. So, do we get any help from this at all?

Derek Burleton: We may. It's a great point. We weren't really talking about that as much before the oil shock. All of a sudden it happens, and that recognition, a reminder of the importance of reliable sources of fossil fuels. Canada accounts for 60% of US crude oil imports.

And you think about it-- I know the president has said we don't need Canada's trees, we don't need Canada's oil. Well, talk to the refining industry in the Gulf Coast or in the Midwest about that and they need the heavy oil from Canada to produce their refineries to set up on that.

So, all that said, you do wonder if this gives Canada a bit more leverage. I wouldn't go too crazy with that analysis, but it doesn't hurt Canada's position in negotiations to try and secure some offsets, be it 232 easing and the like.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, every little bit helps. We'll take it.

Derek Burleton: Absolutely.

Kim Parlee: I've got about a minute, Derek. I want to ask you quickly about currencies. You mentioned with the central banks, we have been pricing in fewer cuts-- sorry, fewer hikes. And then you were saying, later on, we could get some cuts.

How is that going to play out in the currency market? Do you see the Bank of Canada-- is there going to be a divergence in what the central banks are doing, and some changes perhaps?

Derek Burleton: Yeah, I mean, boy, things were so simple ahead of this oil shock because the Fed was viewed as cutting rates twice this year. In fact, that's what we've had in our forecast. And now, all of a sudden, those rate cuts were taken away, as it seems like investors looked at all countries and just raised the bar by 50 basis points.

So in Canada, we had the view-- and we weren't alone-- of no cuts, sort of a stand pat. And all of a sudden, whammo, two to three hikes this year. Same with Europe and the like. So, I think that was very reactionary. In the case of Canada, very aggressive.

So we've seen, of late, a little bit dialing back in that. Two-year yields have pulled back a bit as well. But it's also led to some Canadian dollar weakness.

So, we've had a view for some time of the Canadian dollar rallying to $0.75 at the end of the year. Maybe a bit ambitious. It will depend a lot on how the war goes. But I think right now, it seems like US dollar is getting more inflows.

So much for the death of the US dollar-- it's been grossly exaggerated. It's been strengthening. Canadian dollar has been weakening down to below $0.72 US.

We may see further CAD softening in the near term as some of these rate hikes that we don't believe will happen will get dialed back in Canada. But I think once we get over the hump-- and who knows when that's going to be-- with 75, it may just be a little bit later than the second half of this year that we had initially thought.

Original Post

data.title: Expedia: Margin Expansion Supports Re-Entry Opportunity
data.description:
We were not particularly lucky in buy rating of Expedia Group, Inc. (EXPE) a few weeks before the Middle East conflict. The company's share price declined by more than 15% (Fig. 1), and, in the meantime, Expedia reported its earnings and provided insights into the travel industry. Despite travel disruption and a negative sentiment towards the industry, Q4 numbers provided good support for our expectations. We are taking advantage of this challenging momentum to re-enter Expedia's shares.

For our new readers, we were supportive of the equity story due to 1) outperformance in room nights compared to peers, 2) strong B2B performance, 3) ongoing investments in AI-driven loyalty programs integration, and 4) a valuation discount both on P/E and EV/EBITDA.

Fig 1

Expedia Results and Our Positive Stance

The company reported Q4 gross bookings, top-line sales, and adjusted results. EBITDA at $27 billion, $3.55 billion, and $848 million, respectively. Room nights grew by 9% in Q4 2025 compared with last year (Fig. 2). In Q4, Expedia reported an EBITDA margin of 23.9%, almost 370 basis points higher than Q4 2024.

Thanks to solid FCF generation driven by higher sales and improved operational efficiency, the company's net debt further declined by approximately $1.5 billion.

Fig 2

Why Are We Still Positive?

Starting with Middle East exposure, the company does not break out top-line sales by geography separately. Expedia sales are split 60% in the US and 40% in the Rest of the World (Non-U.S. points of sale - Fig. 3). The Rest of the World accounts for the EU, APAC, and LatAm. If we assume EMEA accounts for 15–20% of the group's sales (even accounting for Trivago), we believe Middle East exposure is a small portion of the company's total sales. It might account for a low single-digit % of Expedia's total top-line sales (≈1–2%). Even if the direct exposure is not material, the impact relates to potential disruptions to global travel flows and pricing dynamics. This is especially true in Air revenue. But again, looking at the top-line sales product, Air sales represent less than 5%.

Fig 3

Thanks to a positive note from Jefferies, we reported that OpenAI is shifting "away from consumer-facing products and its plans to scale back direct checkout ambitions in favor of advertising." At this stage, OpenAI has partnered with Expedia and Booking, allowing users to search for and compare flights and hotels in real time. Again, this reinforced our view that AI chat will be a new sales channel for OTAs.

After the escalation in the Middle East, Expedia stated that "travel management companies (TMCs) are highly optimistic about the next three years of business travel—yet many lack confidence in their ability to meet the rising expectations of today’s business travelers." We're sharing additional supportive datapoints on the broader travel sector. We report that consumer desire for travel remained robust. This was also evident in Q4, with room nights up 9%, driven by high-single-digit growth in the US.

Expedia's key highlight from the Q4 printout, in our view, was the acceleration in B2C performance in the EMEA region. The company reported its strongest growth in nearly three years. Looking at the segment margins, the division also achieved an adjusted EBITDA of 31.5% (Fig. 4), 600 basis points higher than last year. This was reached thanks to 1) marketing efficiencies and 2) a continued expansion in advertising. This will alleviate Wall Street analysts' concerns and serve as a clear catalyst for earnings growth. Additionally, the company beat Wall Street estimates on top-line sales, EBITDA, and EPS.

Fig 4

Q1 2026 gross bookings and top-line sales outlook came in 3% and 4% above Wall Street consensus estimates. Even if the bears may counter that Expedia expects a more muted pace of 2026 EBITDA margin expansion (1-1.25 basis points of higher EBITDA - Fig 5), we report that the company is actively reinvesting in growth initiatives, particularly within the B2B segment, where we believe this will be a key contribution for sustainable profitable growth. B2B bookings once again grew by almost 20%, and the company adds new partners and grows spend per partner. This is key to modeling a better margin evolution ahead.

There is also upside for better margins ahead. Firstly, Expedia highlighted momentum in Rapid API and the related travel agency platform. The company reported that there were more active travel agents than in any prior quarter. Another upside is the evolution of loyalty members. They grew in the mid-single digits, but we report faster growth in the silver tiers and above. This will improve multi-product purchasing and monetization.

Fig 5

Earnings Changes and Valuation

In our last assessment, we already rolled forward our 2026 expectation. We assumed 10% sales growth to $16.2 billion and an EBIT margin of at least 21%. At this stage, we are adjusting our sales growth estimates downwards. We keep the company's core EBIT margin unchanged, which came in aligned with its outlook. So, we forecast top-line sales at $15.9 billion with a core operating profit of $3.34 billion. In our forecast, we assumed Expedia’s B2B segment upside, with double-digit bookings growth. This is driven by share gains with existing partners and higher marketing activity from Expedia’s largest accounts. Expedia's CAPEX-to-sales is usually set at 5%, so we derive a D&A of approximately $750 million. Our implied 2026 EBITDA is set at $4.09 billion. This represents an EBITDA margin of 25.7%, compared to 23.8% reported in 2025. It is higher than the company’s implied forecast (Fig. 5), but execution from Expedia remains impressive, and we believe the 2026 bookings guide could certainly prove conservative. Accounting for lease liabilities, Expedia has a net debt of $1.13 billion, and we are not accounting for the positive FCF evolution in 2026. So, Expedia's enterprise value is $29 billion, and it trades at 7x EV/EBITDA. We continue to value the company at 11.0x EBITDA (in line with the company's historical average). We reduced the company's enterprise value from $45.65 billion to $45 billion. Lowering the EV, given the current net debt, our equity value reaches $43.87 billion. We see a stock price opportunity above 55%. We remain buyers with a target price of $359.5 per share.

Risks

We have long covered Expedia, so we report the following downside risks. Firstly, the company is subject to lower travel demand (conflicts, terrorist attacks, and natural catastrophic events need to be considered when investing in Expedia). Additionally, we report higher inflation that might be detrimental to consumer travel expenditure. We see higher competition within the industry, and AI might be detrimental to the company.

Conclusion

Considering the current escalation (and the minimal exposure to the Middle East), we believe travel demand will likely remain resilient. Expedia reported a solid Q4 printout, with clear signs of improvement in both room nights and operating leverage (demonstrated by its EBITDA growth). Following a 16% decline since our last update (end of January), we believe this is a good time to start/increase a position.

data.title: Hardly Boring: Agentic AI Is Reinvigorating The Legacy Hardware Sector
data.description:
By Yan Taw (YT) Boon

As the artificial intelligence spotlight shifts its gaze from model training to agentic applications, we believe commodity-component suppliers could get a welcome boost.

For the past few years, the semiconductor narrative has largely revolved around one theme: training the large language models at the core of the AI revolution. While capital, talent and investor attention clustered around the most powerful chips, legacy semiconductor and hardware components were left to muddle through a prolonged downcycle.

Now, we believe that bifurcation has begun to unwind: As AI workloads shift from model training to “agentic” applications that can infer what users need and automatically perform those tasks, the action is broadening beyond leading-edge chips to less-glamorous and investment-starved parts of the IT stack.

Even small constraints within these hardware supply chains have the potential to ripple into system-level bottlenecks: Bringing new capacity online can take 2-3 years, and daunting capital expenditures tend to keep new entrants at bay; meanwhile, modern AI infrastructure continues to raise the technical bar for many of these commodity components.

This combination of chronic undersupply and rising performance demands has been pushing hardware prices higher. Given the market’s shifting supply-demand dynamics, we believe this recent recovery in prices is less of a cyclical bounce and more of a longer-term, structural reset.

Greasing the Agentic Gears

Thus far the AI story has been primarily dominated by specialized chips known as "accelerators." These parallel-processing workhouses can perform billions of simultaneous calculations—the raw computational firepower required to train large language AI models. Accelerators live in large data centers filled with clusters of GPUs.

Yet, as AI expands beyond model training into myriad agentic applications, the workloads demand a fundamentally different mix of hardware. Today, millions of users are simultaneously asking AI agents to search the web, compare products, book appointments and execute transactions. Meeting that demand around the clock and at unpredictable scale requires vast fleets of general-purpose components—processors, memory chips and logic components—working in concert with those cutting-edge accelerators.

To keep up, those traditional components now require faster processing speeds, lower latency, greater energy efficiency and tighter manufacturing tolerances. We believe that gap between yesterday’s specifications and today's requirements—exacerbated by the lack of overall hardware supply—is creating a host of potential investment opportunities.

Optical Networking

At the turn of the century, internet scaffolding was built to handle data flowing at 10 to 100 gigabits per second (Gbps), a turtle’s pace by today’s standards. Back then, data centers ran on copper cables, the same electrical wiring technology that underpinned telephone networks. Even as data volumes ballooned, cloud infrastructure generally remained a sleepy corner of the broader tech sector. For example, while copper cables gave way to fiber-optic ones, those quickly became standardized, with manufacturers competing essentially on price.

We believe AI has reinvigorated demand for traditional hardware within the guts of the AI ecosystem.

For example, consider the optical transceivers that convert electrical signals into optical signals. To train large AI models, the industry has had to develop transceivers that can handle 400 to 800 Gbps, many orders of magnitude beyond their legacy counterparts. Now we believe agentic AI is pushing those limits even further, putting the technology on a clear trajectory toward 1.6 to 3.2 terabits per second (Tbps). And as demand for greater data speeds increases exponentially, the cables lacing together all those accelerators, servers and storage systems must now be built to tighter tolerances to preserve signal integrity.

Meanwhile, years of underinvestment in infrastructure hardware have begun to push up prices for various key components: For example, in January 2026, the cost of a single-mode fiber optic cable rose 75% year-on-year, hitting its highest level in seven years.1

Multilayer Ceramic Capacitors

Multilayer ceramic capacitors (MLCCs) might be among the most unglamorous components in electronics. MLCCs protect circuits from electrical spikes and smooth out signal fluctuations, and for most of their commercial history, the core technology changed only incrementally, and suppliers competed mainly on price.

Then came AI. Because the latest GPUs run hotter, faster and at higher power densities, they demand capacitors with dramatically greater energy-storage capacity that can be packed into a much smaller physical space. We believe this is a genuine manufacturing feat that only a handful of suppliers can deliver at scale—a potential bottleneck now being revealed in rising spot prices for MLCCs used in AI, industrial and automotive applications across Taiwan, Korea and China.2

Expanding the AI Opportunity Set

In light of these trends, we believe thematic tech investors should be looking beyond the hyper-scalers and high-performance chipmakers and instead focus more closely on the potential beneficiaries of the broader AI rollout.

Even high-flying Nvidia appears to realize that historically boring parts of the IT stack are no longer an afterthought. In March 2026, CEO Jensen Huang forecasted that Nvidia's annual revenue from AI chips would hit $1 trillion in 2027, up from an estimated $500 billion for 2025 and 2026 combined.3 We believe reaching that number implies massive agentic AI adoption and therefore rising demand for the traditional hardware that took a backseat during the model-training phase.

Putting money where its mouth is, Nvidia invested $2 billion each in Lumentum and Coherent, two makers of optical-networking laser chips, essential components of modern data centers. To us, this $4 billion commitment signals that reliable supply of core optical components may no longer be something that the industry—and tech investors—can take for granted.4

As the AI buildout expands, we believe the spotlight may increasingly fall on the more prosaic parts of the IT stack, where current capacity constraints could bolster pricing across the wider electronics supply chain. In this environment, we think investors shouldn’t be surprised if “legacy” hardware suppliers are rewarded less like late-cycle leftovers and more like scarce inputs.

1 LightReading, “Optical fiber prices are also soaring on AI demand,” February 17, 2026.

2 Passive Components, “MLCC Manufacturers Consider Price Increase as AI Demand Outpaces Supply”, February 18, 2026.

3 The Street, “Nvidia CEO just made a stunning revenue declaration,” March 17, 2026.

4 Nvidia, “NVIDIA Announces Strategic Partnership With Lumentum to Develop State-of-the-Art Optics Technology” and “NVIDIA and Coherent Announce Strategic Partnership to Develop Optics Technology to Scale Next-Generation Data Center Architecture”, March 2, 2026.

If you are an individual retirement investor, contact your financial advisor or other non-Neuberger Berman fiduciary about whether any given investment idea, strategy, product or service described herein may be appropriate for your circumstances.

Nothing herein constitutes a prediction or projection of future events, future markets behaviour, investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell or hold a security. It should not be assumed that any investments in securities, companies, sectors or markets identified and described were or will be profitable. Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
